sentences.text: My name is Henry Rosmarin.
Yes.
R-O-S-M-A-R-I-N.
At birth it was Henryk, H-E-N-R-Y-K Rozmaryn, R-O-S-M-A-R-Y-- I'm sorry, English spelling.
Polish spelling, R-O-Z-M-A-R-Y-N, Rozmaryn.
October 7th, 1925.
My present age is 73.
I was born in a little town called Czeladz, in Poland.
It's southwestern corner of Poland.
I spell it for you, C-- capital C-Z-E-L-A-D-Z.
The nearest town was Bedzin, Sosnowiec.
And towards the German border was Katowice and Siemianowice.
Incidentally, it was in Siemianowice that I lived.
Actually, I was an infant.
My mom told me she went to Czeladz because my father-- my-- my fraternal grandparents lived in Czeladz.
And so this was before hospital births, you know.
This was birth by a midwife, of course.
So she went to Czeladz, which was, like, three kilometers, and gave birth-- birth [INAUDIBLE] to yours truly.
And then she went back when I was probably two weeks old, back to Siemianowice, where I lived until-- for the next few years, of course.
My father's name was Abraham.
Abraham in Polish.
Helena, Helen.
I had one brother, an older brother.
His name was Max, Maximilian.
No.
Later on, shortly before the outbreak of World War II, my father's uncle lived with us for a short while.
His name was-- he was the youngest of my father's brothers.
His name is Benjamin, which was a proper name for the youngest-- for the youngest son, even in biblical times.
Earliest memories?
Childhood, of course-- a happy childhood.
I guess this-- this is true with most of us.
One would say, oh, I had a happy childhood, you know and-- but a carefree childhood, a loving environment, comfortable home.
Parents who cared and showered me with attention-- not only me, both of us, my brother and I.
So I have fond memories of my childhood.
Physical?
Yes, I-- you know, I-- the other night I was thinking about that.
I-- I Sometimes laying in bed at night, I think about, gee, what did my father look like?
You know, I don't-- excuse me.
I don't have a picture.
And I can't-- and every so often I keep just reminding myself and mentally draw a picture, and you see, remember what my father looked like.
My father was of medium height, medium weight.
He never was overweight.
Physically, rather well built.
He was bald.
This I remember distinctly.
He was a muscular man, because of-- I suppose he would develop muscles with the type of work that he did.
And fair skin.
Blue eyes, as a matter of fact.
My later brother looked like him.
He also was blond and blue-eyed, which later on proved to be an asset.
But yes, my father was pretty well-- yeah, he was average-- average height, average build.
And that's as much as physical as I can describe him.
I'm sorry, I don't.
I-- I-- he was born in the late '90s.
He was also born in Czeladz.
Seems like 19-- 18-- I'm sorry, 18-- late 19th century. 1895 or 1897, I don't quite recall.
It's a-- he had a very interesting profession, especially for-- for a member of the Jewish faith.
He was a coal miner, a Jewish coal miner-- perhaps the only one in Poland.
Certainly the only one in that region because I come from a coal mining region.
He was-- he started to work in a coal mine.
Siemianowice was also called Laurahuette, which belonged-- which was part of Germany prior to World War I.
Then as it became Poland, it changed its name from Laurahuette to Siemianowice.
The name of the mine was also [?
Laurahuette-- ?] two names.
It was named after Laura.
He began to work in a coal mine during the German administration.
And he worked for-- I believe he got a gold watch [INAUDIBLE] He [? worked, like, ?] 27 or 28 years in a coal mine until the outbreak of war-- of World War II.
So that was his profession.
He provided very well for us.
Coal miners in those days were paid very well.
The living was above-- above the average worker because they were strongly unionized.
In Polish, it was called 'spuka' and 'bratska,' which means the 'brotherhood of miners.' And he had tremendous benefits to the whole family, of course.
In school I never bought a book, a pencil.
Any kind of textbook, higher education, was provided by the coal mine.
My relationship in my childhood-- my father was, to the-- to the best that I can recall--
We had a very warm relationship, the best I can remember.
Of course, the nature of his work was such that we couldn't-- he worked, as I told you, in a coal mine.
And-- and he would work three shifts.
Then later on, he would work, like, two what they call swing shifts.
If you worked the day shift, which means he was home by 3 or 4 o'clock, we could plan-- like, in the wintertime, he would take me sleigh riding.
I remember him pulling the sleigh and pushing me down the hill.
And in the summertime, he would take me to a soccer game.
So I loved when he was working on the day shift.

But when he worked night shift, which means he had to go to work-- and sleep in the daytime, you know, and go to work, like, in the evening, he would leave home 5, 6-- 6 o'clock, and then I wouldn't see him, perhaps-- because in the morning when he got in, I went to school.
So I would only see him maybe a couple hours in the afternoon.
He naturally would discuss my-- my homework, school, and ask me about my activities.
And we would talk some sports, and-- and probably the usual thing that a father and son [? are ?] doing, with the exception of limited time.
Not so much leisure time, like long weekends, you know?
I must also add he worked, like, a six-day shift, so unfortunately, he had to work on Saturday, half a day.
So those are my recollection about my father.
Summertime on vacation-- we would take vacations together.
Again, the coal miners had a wonderful benefit.
They had what's called a sanatorium.
It's a place where they would give, I think, something like four to six weeks, depending on one's health.

And the length of time he worked in the mines, he would get, like, a respite period in the mountains of Poland, mountains and also close to the forest, to the, like, pine forest where the air was supposedly cleaner and better for the lungs.
Because, you know, coal miners did suffer from lung diseases.
So that we would-- when he would be sent to this sanatorium, the whole family would-- would go along.
We would rent a place, like, a couple rooms not far from him, and my dad would be in a sanatorium.
We would meet for meals together, you know, we'll go out on the town.
And so vacations we would spend together.
How would you describe your mother?
My mother, blessed memory.
She was everything a child can dream of-- can dream about having, all-- all put in one little package.
She was kind of short.
In English, in our terms, she would be, perhaps, 5', 5' 1'.
Dark hair, dark brown eyes.
Always smiling, at least most of the time.
Very musical.
[INAUDIBLE] had a pretty good voice.
She sang Yiddish songs, Polish songs, [COUGH], sometimes German songs.
Mostly Yiddish and Polish, however.
But if she liked a song, it didn't matter what-- what the lyrics were.
She would sing, starting with lullabies, of course, and later on-- I always remember her-- if she did her housework, she always would hum or sing.
She was a homemaker.
Perhaps-- I try to think of a fancier title for-- for-- for a real-- for real good homemaker.
The house was-- the apartment was immaculately clean.
She would do all of the work.
Didn't have any domestic help.
She would do all of her work, and didn't have a laundromat at home to-- to-- to do the laundry.
But she did all of the house chores herself.
A tremendous cook-- baker, cook.
And above all, besides the domestic description I give of my mother, a very loving and caring mother.
I-- I don't remember waking up with just say, Henrik, get up, it's time to go to school.
It was always with a kiss.
And sometimes-- I remember when I was smaller, I used to wake up to a warm hugging.
And-- and I would wipe my eyes quick, and then it-- I felt some moisture on my eyes until I found out she actually kissed and licked my eyelids to wake me up.
And sometimes I think about that.
What expression of love.
When I see some-- a kitten-- a kitten doing this, I think-- I think about what my mom did.
And I can't say enough about both of my parents.
I just wished I had the gift of having them around longer.
But the environment at home was excellent, warm, loving, caring.
They gave me a childhood like no child could ask for anything better.
Material things, they did the best they could.
But in terms of attention and love, it was much more than one could ask for.
Yeah.
My brother and I were, perhaps, opposite in terms of interest.
Our characters were the same.
And of course, we grew up in the same environment.
His interest-- he was a more serious type.
If he would grow in this country, he probably would've been in advanced classes and learning in school.
He was always ahead.
The difference in our age was, like, 20 months-- actually, let's see, 18 months difference in ages.
He was 18 months older, and yet he was much more serious.
I was the typical kid with the soccer and, kind of, activities in school, and mischievous.

But he was a more serious type-- books that I've heard of-- he was in fourth and fifth grade, he was reading Tolstoy and Victor Hugo, and poetry by Polish Adam Mickiewicz, Henryk Sienkiewicz-- stuff that I wouldn't read until I got into eighth-- eighth grade.
And he was way ahead.
Scholarly, much, much advanced for his age.
At home, sometimes I would resent, because the other kids had brothers they would play soccer with, you know, or play a game of chess, checkers-- not chess, checkers.
And he-- he would rather take a book or a newspaper or serious books and read.
And I would say, let's go outside.
Let's kick some ball.
Then as he would kick, he would kick it kind of awkward, you know, and I would make fun out of him.
But he could make fun out of me when it came to the [? scholarly. ?] So I would say, gosh, why can't you do like the other brothers?
But he was especially gifted.
What language did you speak at home?
It's a very interesting question.
It's an interesting concept, too.
We spoke at home Yiddish mixed with Polish sometimes.
My father was fluent in German, because he worked under the German administration.
And I must also say he was pro-German, as far as his political leanings.
As soon as I stepped outside the [? trestle ?] and closed the door and I was outside, like, I would go to a different country or another planet.
Yiddish was forgotten altogether.
It was switched over to Polish.
No sooner the door closed behind me, I was already thinking in Polish, answering in Polish.
And if my mom would ever make a mistake and call me for dinner and call me in Yiddish, I wanted to bury my head, you know, in the sand, because why didn't she say it in Polish?
Because the kids would make fun.
So to answer your question, the language at home, inside, within our own confinement-- yes, Yiddish.
Outside, Polish.
And with my neighbors, German sometimes, because these were people who hardly knew Polish.
Poland only took over that territory eight or nine years before my time, so obviously they spoke German.
So I would speak with them German, as well.
But at home, Yiddish.
How would I describe--
With neighbors.
Very-- also coal miners.
You see, the union was called brotherhood, but it also spilled over to the spouses and to the children and family.
We were very close-knit families.
They were all coal miners.
So my mother would visit with them, they would visit in our house.

Sometimes I remember dad, when I go to sleep, he would get-- he would get irritated because some neighbor would come in and yak and yak and tell us stories about [COUGHS] her husband and about domestic stories, you know, complaining.
And my mom used to always listen.
And because she had such a sympathetic ear for everyone, most people would sit around-- this is before TV, you know.
So they would come in and sip some tea or coffee and talk about the domestic problem.
And my mom would listen, like a confessional, you know.
So the neighbors-- we had a very close relation, of course, and I played with their kids, as well.

So our neighbors were very, very friendly, and we had common grounds of interest because, again, families of coal miners share the same interest, with the exception of, you know, obviously I was Jewish.
But when it comes to, you know-- except on Sundays or Shabbats, we would part ways.
But other than that, we had common interest.
So it was a very-- very congenial, very, I must say, friendly atmosphere.
And these were tenement houses owned by the mines, by the coal mines.
Very modern houses for [BACKGROUND NOISE] Polish because this was German territory.
And Germany, as you know, was much more developed.
Their buildings were more modern, westernized.
Water was inside water.
Where in Czeladz, for instance, which was Poland proper, I remember going to the pump and pumping water.
We had indoor water and indoor plumbing.
Not a private bathroom, but in a hallway.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Each two apartments had one bathroom.

So as far as the standards in those days, which were probably higher standards, the apartments-- the tenement houses were, probably, I want to guess, I'd say four stories high for about 16 tenements in a house, in a building.
Comfortable buildings, comfortable rooms.
Not a whole lot of rooms.
We had [INAUDIBLE] a foyer, a big kitchen, and a big living room, which-- actually, a big bedroom, actually, because the kitchen served as a place to eat.
It was a huge-- huge apartment.
No, we shared.
I didn't have my own room.
We shared.
That would have been considered luxury in those days in Poland.
Very, very, very well, except he read a lot at night.
He kept me up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was reading, too, but I would read sport pages, and he would read more serious stuff.
We had a wonderful relationship as far as brothers.
It's the kind of relationship, I think-- almost if I would cut myself, he would bleed, or vice versa.
It was very close.
My favorite part of the house was the part where in the living room, in the particular side of the living room, we had a big radio.
My uncle who lived with us two or three years before the war, he was a salesman.
He was selling electronics.
And a radio in those days was-- was the-- was almost like digital, or I don't know what the most modern TV is today, but-- but it was-- a radio was-- not everyone had a radio.
We're talking mid-30s, you know.
But he was in the business and so he-- he-- I remember, he bought a big console, which three bands, three or four bands.
And I would listen to-- I would listen to music from Germany, from Czechoslovakia, in different languages.
Especially on short wave I would catch it.
Sometimes BBC from England.
I didn't understand a word what they were saying, but I would listen to the music.
And I remember my first introduction to the upbeat music.
I remember Benny Goodman.
I had no idea who they were, but the music was almost like a signature, and i could tell, this has-- it's got to be Benny Goodman or Glenn Miller, you know?

Later on, the names became more-- more familiar, but until I heard-- I heard the music, it was really strange because I was used to listening to either Polish stations or German stations, with the marches, you know, and Wagnerian music.
So-- and this all of a sudden I could tap my foot to.
And all of a sudden, I felt, you know, [? very ?] happy.
So that was my favorite part of the-- of the [INAUDIBLE].
Worked the dial, you know.
And I used to imitate different stations and different announcers, where he had different languages.
Didn't know what they were saying, but the sound, you know.
I remember listening to the Czech stations, and I will announce what now we would call station break.
They would say, radio--
[SPEAKING POLISH] I used to imitate it.
I thought it sounded funny.
But those are fond memories of childhood and what you could do.
And you asked me about favorite room, this was it.
Soccer games were not broadcast very often, unless they were international games.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Don't get me started on [? enchanted ?] subject, I-- we'll spend a-- we'll spend a week here.
Did the family--
Yes.
Oh, especially my dad because my mom had her own program.
I'm glad you asked that question because I was going to tell you about-- she-- first of all, my mom.
She had a program.
It was called, in Polish, [POLISH].
Translated, it means wish-- the wishes-- the [? wishes ?] concert.
They would play a song and this dedication-- with dedications.
Someone would call up, you know-- or you couldn't call up, but write into the station.
This is not like talk radio today.
And they had, like, live shows, from a cabaret, you know.
And so those were her favorite.
My dad loved a broadcast in Yiddish.
The Yiddish broadcast came from the Russian and the Ukraine.
And I remember when I would be doing my homework or try to read something, I would-- he would turn it up a little louder.
And I remember the sound of the 'Internationale,' you know-- [HUMMING 'THE INTERNATIONALE'].
And so here comes-- here comes a broadcast.
And it would come on, and my dad would be glued to that, you know.
He wants to crawl into that console.
And the announcer would start out with a greeting of the typical proletarian greetings in Yiddish.
[SPEAKING YIDDISH] 'Unite.
Workers unite.' And my father was fascinated that there's a Yiddish program coming out of a Russian republic-- the USSR then.
And of course, what it was is propaganda, you know.
Because he was a coal miner, naturally, he would lean towards the workers.
And he would, you know, dream of a workers' paradise and quote some of the-- some of the ideology which he heard.
And he said, this is-- this is the way it should be, you know, and all that.
So this was his favorite broadcast.
